name: Fides
description: Fides, in Roman mythology, is the goddess of good faith, trust, and oaths. She embodies the essential virtue of keeping one's word and upholding agreements, both in personal and public life. Fides was considered a crucial foundation for Roman society and its legal system. Her importance stemmed from the belief that without trust and good faith, no contracts, treaties, or social bonds could be reliably maintained. She was seen as a guardian of justice and fair dealing, ensuring that promises were honored and obligations fulfilled. Her worship was closely tied to the state, particularly the Senate, which placed great emphasis on maintaining Fides within its dealings with other nations. Temples dedicated to Fides were often located near important public spaces, such as the Roman Forum, highlighting her centrality to the civic life of Rome. The concept of Fides extended beyond mere honesty; it encompassed a sense of loyalty, faithfulness, and integrity in all aspects of life. Violating Fides was considered a grave offense, carrying significant social and religious consequences. Fides was not merely a passive virtue; she was an active force that influenced human behavior, encouraging individuals to act with honor and trustworthiness. Her presence was invoked in oaths and agreements, serving as a divine witness to the commitments made. In essence, Fides represented the moral compass of Roman society, guiding individuals and institutions towards ethical conduct and mutual respect. She ensured that the Roman state and its citizens acted with integrity, both internally and in their interactions with the wider world. Her influence permeated all levels of society, from the humblest citizen to the most powerful senator.
subtitle: Goddess of Trust and Good Faith

keyMyths.title: Temple Dedication
keyMyths.description: The first temple to Fides was dedicated by Numa Pompilius, the second king of Rome, signifying the early importance of trust in Roman society. This act established Fides as a crucial deity within the Roman pantheon.

keyMyths.title: Senate and Fides
keyMyths.description: The Senate often met in the Temple of Fides to discuss treaties and foreign relations, emphasizing the link between good faith and Rome's international dealings. This highlighted the importance of upholding agreements with other nations.

keyMyths.title: Oaths and Fides
keyMyths.description: Oaths sworn in the name of Fides were considered particularly binding, reflecting the goddess's role as a guarantor of truthfulness and integrity. Violating such oaths was seen as a serious offense against both the gods and the state.

sources.author: Livy
sources.description: A comprehensive history of Rome from its founding.
sources.text: A comprehensive history of Rome from its founding.

sources.author: Cicero
sources.description: A treatise on moral duties and ethics in Roman society.
